lesson.menu_desc: Wish / if only + third conditional for arguing the court got it wrong -- 29 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 15 -- The Appeal: arguing the lower court erred and asking a higher court to overturn it.
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Luiz Bressane | Aula 15 -- The Appeal

listenings.text: An appeal is not a second trial, and that is the first thing a young defender must understand. You are not arguing the facts again; you are arguing the law. Find the error. If the judge had excluded that confession, the jury would never have heard it. If only the trial counsel had objected at the right moment, the record would now protect your client. So you build your grounds: an error of law, a misdirection to the jury, a verdict against the weight of the evidence. Then you ask the appellate court for one of three things. Quash the conviction and acquit. Overturn it and order a fresh trial. Or remit the case with directions. Be honest about the strong grounds and abandon the weak ones, because the appellate bench has read the file, and credibility is all you have on appeal.

You are listening to the legal affairs review. The Court of Appeal today delivered its judgment in the case of the State versus Andrade. The appellant had argued that the trial judge wrongly admitted a confession obtained without counsel, and that this amounted to a miscarriage of justice. The respondent urged the court to uphold the conviction, insisting the evidence was overwhelming regardless. After two days of argument, the appellate judges agreed that an error of law had occurred. They quashed the conviction and remitted the case for a fresh trial before a different judge. The appellant's counsel said afterwards that, had the confession been excluded at trial, his client would never have been convicted in the first place. We will follow the retrial closely.
